title: Abhinav Chand
text:
I am an Assistant Professor in the Mathematics Department at the University of Wisconsin-Eau Claire. I teach data science and mathematics courses.
Currently teaching:

DS 150 Computing in Python: Fundamentals and Procedural Programming
DS 740 Data Mining & Machine Learning

My current research interests are as follows:

Network Science
Graph Neural Networks and Transformers
Machine learning
Mechanistic Interpretability

Teaching philosophy TLDR:

Develop the habit of learning from first principles

Motivate everything
Well motivated and engaging active learning problem sets with appropriate scaffolding (TILT)

Students build things that they are passionate about through mini-projects and capstone projects

A functional and affordable AI tutor

Teaching stack: Mentimeter, Manim, Nbgrader, markdown2canvas, codeshare, Slack

title: Publications
text:
References

Albin, Nathan, Brian Benson, Abhinav Chand, and Pietro Poggi-Corradini. 2025. “The Spanning Tree Modulus and the Laplace Spectrum of a Graph.” Unpublished Manuscript, November.


Chand, Abhinav. 2025. “Influence Problems on a Content-Spreading Model and Graph Machine Learning.” Phys. Rev. E 112 (December): 064312. https://doi.org/10.1103/2cnl-b6wl.


Chand, Abhinav, Leonel Robert, and Arindam Sutradhar. 2021. “Simultaneous Averaging to Zero by Unitary Mixing Operators.” Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society 149 (12): 5169–77. https://doi.org/10.1090/proc/15495.

title: Math 250: Applied Calculus (Spring 2021)

title: Math 250: Applied Calculus (Spring 2021)
text:
Lecture Notes
Jan 13: Functions and rate of change Jan 21: Functions in economics Jan 26: Exponential Functions Jan 28: Natural log Feb 4: Proportionality and powers + Review Feb 10: Velocity graph Feb 18: Derivative Feb 23: Derivative Feb 25: Marginal cost and revenue Mar 9: Derivative for powers and polynomials March 11: Chainrule March 16: Product and quotient rules + Local min and max March 18: First and second derivative test March 23: Inflection points and global maxima minima March 25: Profit cost and revenue April 8: Accumulated change as definite integral April 13: Definite integral as areas April 15: Fundamental theorem of calculus April 20: Antiderivate and applications April 22: Review April 29: Final review

Exams

title: Precalculus Algebra
text: This is not the official course page. Some materials for the course are listed below.

title: Precalculus Algebra
text:
Lecture Notes
1. Why calculus? 2. Graph of a function 3. Composing functions 4. One-to-one and inverse 5. Polynomials 6. Division of polynomials 7. Zeros and end behavior 8. Rational functions 8.1 Factor theorem, Descartes’ rule of signs 9. Complex numbers 10. Exponents 11. Logarithms 10. More on logarithms 12. Exp and log models 12. Exponents and Logarithm equations 13. System of equations 14. Three variables 15. Gauss Jordan

title: Precalculus Algebra
text:
Exams
1. Midterm 1  (Solution) 2. Midterm 2 (Solution) 3. Midterm 3 (Solution) 4. Midterm 4 5. Final

title: Math 340: Differential Equations (Summer 2024)
text: This course teaches you the fundamentals of differential equations. Differential equations started in the works of Newton and Leibniz. They were trying to describe motion of bodies under the influence of force and how the motion evolved with time. Differential equations describe how a quantity of interest, such as displacement,velocity, heat, population etc. evolves with time.

title: Math 340: Differential Equations (Summer 2024)
text: This course teaches you the fundamentals of differential equations. Differential equations started in the works of Newton and Leibniz. They were trying to describe motion of bodies under the influence of force and how the motion evolved with time. Differential equations describe how a quantity of interest, such as displacement,velocity, heat, population etc. evolves with time.

title: Math 340: Differential Equations (Summer 2024)
text:
Syllabus
Course Syllabus

title: Math 340: Differential Equations (Summer 2024)
text:
Instructor Details
Lecturer and Coordinator: Abhinav Chand
Email: achand1@{} #put ksu.edu inside the brakcets and remove the brackets.
Office: Cardwell Hall 127
Office hours: MTWU 9:15-10:15 pm or by appointment (email to schedule). Also available by zoom.

title: Math 340: Differential Equations (Summer 2024)
text:
Course Objectives

Learn how to solve first order linear ODE.
Learn how to plot slope fields.
Learn how to approximate solutions to differential equations using Euler’s method.
Learn how to classify differential equations.
Learn how to solve second order linear ODEs with constant coefficients.
Learn about higher order linear ODEs and their matrix form.
Learn about mechanical vibrations and how to solve them.
Learn how to solve system of ODEs using eigenvalue method
Learn how to draw phase diagram of linear ODE.
Learn how to solve differential equations using Laplace transform.

title: Math 340: Differential Equations (Summer 2024)
text:
Required Texts
We will use Notes on Diffy Qs: Differential Equations for Engineers by Jiri Lebl. The textbook is available free of charge.

title: Math 340: Differential Equations (Summer 2024)
text:
Resources
You should contact your instructor for any exceptional circumstances. We encourage you to seek help from the instructor during their office hours.
The math help session is a great resource for math tutoring and are held Monday through Friday, beginning the second week of class. Several instructors will be present to assist you. Math Help
Other K-state Tutoring: Academic Achievement Center Tutoring Services
Steve Brunton ODE videos: Differential Equations.
3Blue1Brown Calculus videos: Essence of Calculus.
3Blue1Brown Linear Algebra videos: Essence of Linear Algebra.
